metadata.from: Bob (Robert) Hanifen — signed 'Bob', return address matches Cpl. Robert Hanifen on envelope
metadata.to: Hanifen Body & Paint Co. (Dad/James Edward Hanifen Sr.), Des Moines, Iowa
metadata.location_written_from: Greensboro, N.C. (331 North Elm Street — appears to be a telephone company/Bell System letterhead)
metadata.location_sent_to: Hanifen Body & Paint Co., P.O. Box [illegible], Des Moines, Iowa
metadata.envelope_notes: Return address: Cpl. Robert Hanifen, #17170600, Squadron 'O' Branch #2, HAFORD [possibly AAFRD?], Greensboro, N.C. Stamp area marked 'Null' with what appears to be a 'Free' franking notation. Note: postmark date (Jan 18) appears to conflict with letter date (Jan 29) — possibly the envelope was reused or the letter date is in error.
metadata.mood_tone: Upbeat and matter-of-fact, with a sense of excitement and urgency; a farewell tone as Bob is shipping out overseas
metadata.legibility_notes: Heavy folding creases obscure some words; ink bleed-through from reverse side visible on page 2; some words difficult to parse due to cursive style. Postmark date (Jan 18) conflicts with letter date (Jan 29) — may indicate the envelope was pre-dated or reused.
metadata.historical_context: Post-WWII troop redistribution and deployment; Bob is shipping out from Camp Kilmer, NJ (a major WWII embarkation point) to Naples, Italy, likely as part of post-war occupation forces. Reference to troops being sent to Germany (Hamburg) for occupation duty. The letter reflects the large-scale movement of American forces in early 1946.

pages.transcription:
[Return address - upper left, in reddish/brown ink]
Cpl. Robert Hanifen #17170600
Sqadron 'O' Branch #2
HAFORD
Greensboro, N.C.

[Postmark - center]
GREENSBORO
JAN 18
3 PM
1946
N.C.

[Stamp area - upper right]
Null [franking notation]

[Addressee - center, in black ink]
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
P.O. Box [illegible]
Des Moines
Iowa

[Letterhead: 331 NORTH ELM STREET — telephone center — GREENSBORO, N. C. (Bell System logo)]

Jan. 29

Dear Dad

We are finally leaving Greensboro. We leave at 8:30 PM the 29 for Camp Kilmer N.J. ersey, it is just 30 min. out of New York City. Hope we get a pass we are supposed to be there 48 hours. 23 hundred of us on the boat. We are going Naples, Italy. Not too bad. Don Pilant is going Germany probably Hamburg. Wish I was going there. We will probably go into Germany somewhere

after a while. Naples is for redistribution to different areas. I received the 26 Saturday they had everything mixed up. I will [write?] you from Camp Kilmer if I can. Well I got to run now. So goodby for now

Lots of Love
Bob

full_transcription:
Jan. 29

Dear Dad

We are finally leaving Greensboro. We leave at 8:30 PM the 29 for Camp Kilmer N.J. ersey, it is just 30 min. out of New York City. Hope we get a pass we are supposed to be there 48 hours. 23 hundred of us on the boat. We are going Naples, Italy. Not too bad. Don Pilant is going Germany probably Hamburg. Wish I was going there. We will probably go into Germany somewhere after a while. Naples is for redistribution to different areas. I received the 26 Saturday they had everything mixed up. I will [write?] you from Camp Kilmer if I can. Well I got to run now. So goodby for now

Lots of Love
Bob
summary: Written January 29, 1946, Bob informs his father that he is finally leaving Greensboro, North Carolina, heading to Camp Kilmer, New Jersey — a major embarkation point just outside New York City — before shipping out overseas with 2,300 fellow soldiers. His destination is Naples, Italy, which he describes as a redistribution point before likely being sent into Germany. He mentions that his comrade Don Pilant is heading directly to Germany, possibly Hamburg, and Bob wishes he were going there instead. The letter is a brief, rushed farewell written just before departure.
